annotations.label: Dehn's solution to Hilbert's third problem

anchor.section: Nature and influence of the problems
anchor.snippet: the 3rd problem, which was the first to be solved

annotations.note: Mathlib has general equidecomposability infrastructure under group actions but no Dehn invariant or the scissors-congruence (non-)theorem for polyhedra.

annotations.label: Gödel's second incompleteness theorem

anchor.snippet: Gödel's second incompleteness theorem gives a precise sense in which such a finitistic proof of the consistency of arithmetic is provably impossible

annotations.note: Only arithmetization tools like the Gödel β-function and first-order ModelTheory exist; neither incompleteness theorem is stated or proven.

annotations.label: Undecidability of Hilbert's tenth problem

anchor.snippet: this problem was solved by showing that there cannot be any such algorithm

annotations.note: Matiyasevich's key step (the power function is Diophantine) is formalized, but the file's TODO notes the full undecidability/unsolvability conclusion is not yet completed.

annotations.label: Independence of the continuum hypothesis (Cohen)

anchor.section: Nature and influence of the problems
anchor.snippet: Paul Cohen received the Fields Medal in 1966 for his work on the first problem

annotations.note: Mathlib has aleph/beth cardinal functions and mentions GCH only in docstrings, but has no forcing machinery or independence-of-CH result.

annotations.label: Gleason's solution to Hilbert's fifth problem
annotations.note: No formalization of the Gleason–Montgomery–Zippin solution that locally Euclidean topological groups are Lie groups.

annotations.label: Hilbert–Smith conjecture

anchor.snippet: the problem becomes the Hilbert–Smith conjecture

annotations.note: The Hilbert–Smith conjecture is neither stated nor proven in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Kolmogorov's axiomatization of probability

anchor.snippet: Kolmogorov's axiomatics are accepted as the foundation of probability theory

annotations.note: Mathlib's measure-theoretic probability is built on Kolmogorov's axioms, with a probability measure formalized as a countably additive measure of total mass one.

annotations.label: Kolmogorov–Arnold representation theorem

anchor.snippet: the Kolmogorov–Arnold representation theorem shows that every multivariate continuous function can be obtained through such composition

annotations.note: No formalization of the superposition/representation theorem for multivariate continuous functions.

annotations.label: Derivation of fluid and Boltzmann equations (Deng–Hani–Ma)

anchor.snippet: deriving continuous fluid equations and Boltzmann equations from Newton's laws applied for particles

annotations.note: Mathlib has nothing on Boltzmann/fluid equations or their derivation from particle dynamics.

annotations.label: Solution of Schubert's characteristic problem

anchor.snippet: Schubert's characteristic problem has been solved by Haibao Duan and Xuezhi Zhao

annotations.note: No formalization of Schubert's enumerative characteristic problem.

annotations.label: Dwork's proof of the first Weil conjecture

anchor.snippet: The first conjecture was proven by Bernard Dwork

annotations.note: The Weil conjectures and Dwork's rationality theorem for zeta functions of varieties are absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Deligne's proof of the Riemann hypothesis analogue

anchor.snippet: The last and deepest of the Weil conjectures, an analogue of the Riemann hypothesis, was proven by Pierre Deligne

annotations.note: The Riemann hypothesis analogue of the Weil conjectures is not formalized (the 'Deligne' files in Mathlib concern only Gamma-factor identities).

annotations.label: Riemann hypothesis

anchor.snippet: The Riemann hypothesis is noteworthy for its appearance on the list of Hilbert problems

annotations.note: The statement is formalized as the Prop `RiemannHypothesis` asserting nontrivial zeros of riemannZeta lie on Re s = 1/2 (statement only, unproven).

annotations.label: Resolution of the Poincaré conjecture

anchor.snippet: one of the prize problems (the Poincaré conjecture ) was solved relatively soon after the problems were announced

annotations.note: The 3-dimensional Poincaré conjecture statement is formalized but only as a `proof_wanted` declaration with no proof in Mathlib.
